data.title: Allianz to acquire HSBC’s Singapore insurance unit for $2.09B
data.description:
• Allianz Group has agreed to acquire HSBC’s (HSBC) Singapore life insurance unit for 2.7 billion Singapore dollars ($2.09 billion), expanding the company’s footprint in Asia’s life and health insurance market.
  • The German insurer expects to generate a double-digit return on investment in the medium-term, and the transaction is expected to close in the first half of 2027.
  • As part of the deal, Allianz will also enter a 15-year exclusive distribution partnership with HSBC Singapore (HSBC) to strengthen its ties in the region.
  • Allianz aims “to support more individuals and communities even more comprehensively, with a broader product portfolio that helps protect and plan for what matters most to them,” said Renate Wagner, a member of the board of management. 
  • HSBC Life Singapore generated an operating profit of 80 million euros ($91 million) in 2025.

data.title: Japan's core CPI rebounds to 1.6% in June; annual inflation rises to 1.7%
MaxsMaking gets delisting notice from Nasdaq
data.description:
• MaxsMaking (MAMK) received a Nasdaq staff delisting determination after an SEC temporary trading suspension on Nov. 17, 2025, after which Nasdaq halted trading in MAMK shares on Dec. 2, 2025.
  • Nasdaq said the stock was susceptible to third-party price manipulation, indicating insufficient liquidity to support fair and orderly markets.
  • The exchange said delisting is consistent with investor protection and the public interest.
  • MaxsMaking (MAMK) plans to request a hearing before an independent Nasdaq Hearings Panel.

data.title: Avidia Bancorp, Inc. GAAP EPS of $0.39, revenue of $29.86M
data.description:
• Avidia Bancorp, Inc. press release (AVBC): Q2 GAAP EPS of $0.39.
  • Revenue of $29.86M.

  • The period-end allowance for credit losses was $23.9 million in the second quarter of 2026 compared to $22.8 million at the prior quarter-end. The ratio of the allowance to total loans increased to 1.06% from 1.00% for these dates.

    • Provision expense for credit losses decreased $197 thousand to $892 thousand.
    • The Company recorded a benefit to the allowance of $420 thousand for net recoveries in the second quarter, compared to net charge-offs of $116 thousand recorded in the prior quarter.
    • Non-accruing loans were $16.9 million, or 0.75% of total loans, at period-end, compared to $13.6 million, or 0.60% of total loans, at the prior quarter-end, primarily due to an increase in non-accruing commercial & industrial loans.

data.title: Scholastic projects 2%-4% revenue growth and $135M-$145M adjusted EBITDA in fiscal 2027, following 25% dividend increase
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Scholastic Corporation (SCHL) Q4 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • "Fiscal 2026 was an important year for Scholastic. It demonstrated the earnings power of the more focused company we've been building" (President, CEO & Director Peter Warwick), while also emphasizing capital actions: "The sale-leaseback transactions unlocked over $400 million in net proceeds" and "During the year, we returned over $285 million to shareholders."

  • On operating drivers, Warwick highlighted the mix of strengths and headwinds: "Book Fairs continued to perform well. Entertainment returned to growth, and education showed improving trends even as headwinds continued."

  • Glover summarized the quarter and full-year topline: "Revenues decreased 6% to $476.1 million in the fourth quarter and 3% to $1.6 billion in the full fiscal year" (CFO & Executive Vice President Haji Glover).

  • Glover tied profitability and EPS to portfolio performance and capital actions: "Adjusted net income was $45.9 million in the fourth quarter" and "On a per diluted share basis, adjusted earnings were $2.19 compared to $0.87 last year," adding that "fewer shares outstanding following share repurchase activity also benefited adjusted earnings per share."

Outlook

  • "For fiscal 2027, we expect revenue growth of approximately 2% to 4% compared to fiscal 2026 reported revenue and adjusted EBITDA of approximately $135 million to $145 million" (CFO Glover).

  • On quarterly cadence, Glover said, "we expect first quarter revenues to be slightly down versus prior year period" and "Year-over-year revenue growth is expected to begin in the second quarter and continue through the balance of the year," while also flagging profitability seasonality: "We also expect a seasonal operating loss modestly greater than the prior year period, primarily reflecting the sale leaseback impact."

  • On cash generation, Glover guided: "Our outlook for free cash flow in fiscal 2027 is approximately $35 million to $40 million."

Financial Results

  • Glover reported profitability metrics and sale-leaseback comparability framing: "Adjusted EBITDA was $84.7 million in the fourth quarter compared to $91.2 million in the prior year period" and "On the same comparable basis, fourth quarter adjusted EBITDA increased $1 million."

  • In Children’s Book Publishing and Distribution, Glover said, "revenues for the fourth quarter decreased 4% to $276.3 million," while Book Fairs grew: "Book Fairs revenues increased 5% in the fourth quarter to $186.6 million" and "over 103,000 case and shippable fairs held during the fiscal year."

  • Education remained pressured: "fourth quarter revenues were $109.2 million, down 13%" and "full year revenues were $267.6 million, a decrease of 14%" (CFO Glover), with the driver stated as "ongoing funding volatility."

  • Entertainment showed sharp Q4 growth: "fourth quarter revenues increased 42% to $21 million" and "Segment adjusted operating income improved $2.9 million to $0.8 million in the fourth quarter compared to a loss of $2.1 million a year ago" (CFO Glover).

Q&A

  • Brendan McCarthy, Sidoti & Company, LLC: "That 3% decline came in a little bit under the expectations and the guidance for the year. Just what were the key variables there that drove that impact?" President Warwick: "the 2 main factors were" the prior-year comp from "Sunrise on the Reaping" and that "we also didn't get as much revenue from the education segment as we were expecting."

  • McCarthy, Sidoti & Company, LLC: "Was that really driven by higher fair count? Or did more of that growth come from better economics and revenue per fair?" President Warwick: "It was primarily driven by the number of fairs" and "we also saw a modest increase in revenue per fair as well," adding "much more adoption of e-wallets" and "a really good end of the year in our sponsored fairs."

  • McCarthy, Sidoti & Company, LLC: "do you see improved unit economics" and "what's the expectation for total fair count" in fiscal 2027? President Warwick: "we expect fair count to increase in fiscal 2027" and "We'll expect to see revenue per fair grow maybe" similarly to fiscal 2026.

  • McCarthy, Sidoti & Company, LLC: "What's the outlook" for Education in fiscal 2027 and what supports stabilization? President Warwick: "we have a stronger product portfolio" and "a new Chief Revenue Officer, who joined us during the fourth quarter," adding "we feel pretty confident" that performance improves "over the full course of the year."

  • McCarthy, Sidoti & Company, LLC: "What kind of maybe revenue run rate" is needed for sustained Entertainment profitability? President Warwick: "we've got very strong visibility" and "we're already at over 100% of last year's revenues" in contracted revenues.

  • Andrew Crum, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: "how would you assess the health of your consumer?" President Warwick: "we are feeling pretty good" citing "the strength of our Publishing" and "Book Fair, bookings are good."

  • Crum, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: "are you assuming revenue can grow year-on-year in 3Q, 4Q" for Education? President Warwick: "the assumption that you're making... is correct" and "you'll be able to see that in the second half of the year."

  • Crum, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: "fund buybacks with cash" or use the revolver? CFO Glover: "we are strong on cash" and "we'll pull from both sources if needed," adding "we feel very confident we have enough cash to fund our $183 million of potential opportunity."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing on revenue/education visibility, using framing such as "under the expectations" and probing for quantification: "maybe quantify what that ultimately looks like" (Brendan McCarthy, Sidoti & Company, LLC).

  • Management tone was slightly positive and confidence-leaning on core channels and FY27 execution, using language including "we feel pretty confident" (President Warwick) and "we've got very strong visibility" (President Warwick), while also narrowing assumptions: "Our outlook does not currently assume a material impact from additional tariff actions" (CFO Glover).

  • Versus the prior quarter’s heavier balance-sheet/tender-offer emphasis ("the Board has authorized a $300 million share repurchase authorization" (President Warwick, Q3)), Q4 language shifted toward operating execution: "Fiscal 2027 is an execution year" (President Warwick).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Capital return messaging evolved from announcing the tender framework in Q3 ("a $200 million modified Dutch Auction tender offer" (President Warwick, Q3)) to reporting outcomes in Q4: "we returned over $285 million to shareholders" and "repurchase of approximately 7.3 million shares... for $268.6 million" (CFO Glover, Q4).

  • Guidance posture shifted from Q3 reaffirmation ("we're reaffirming our fiscal 2026 adjusted EBITDA and free cash flow guidance" (President Warwick, Q3)) to Q4 issuing FY27 targets: "revenue growth of approximately 2% to 4%" and "adjusted EBITDA of approximately $135 million to $145 million" (CFO Glover, Q4).

  • Education narrative moved from Q3 deceleration optimism ("Revenues were down 2%" (President Warwick, Q3)) to Q4 acknowledging larger declines alongside a second-half trend: "the rate of decline decelerated in the second half of fiscal 2026" (CFO Glover, Q4).

Risks and Concerns

  • Funding volatility in Education remained the central operational risk: "education continues to operate in a pressured purchasing environment" (President Warwick) and "school and district funding conditions remain volatile" (CFO Glover).

  • Trade and International comparisons were called out as headwinds tied to prior-year timing: "a challenging comparison with the prior year fourth quarter publishing schedule" (CFO Glover).

  • Tariff assumptions were bounded but still a watch item: "Our outlook reflects current tariff rates" and "Our outlook does not currently assume a material impact from additional tariff actions" (CFO Glover).

Final Takeaway

Management framed fiscal 2026 as the proof point of a more focused model and a reshaped balance sheet, highlighted by sale-leaseback proceeds and accelerated shareholder returns, while positioning fiscal 2027 as an execution year with guided revenue growth of approximately 2% to 4%, adjusted EBITDA of approximately $135 million to $145 million, and free cash flow of approximately $35 million to $40 million, led by continued Book Fairs momentum, a stronger trade pipeline tied to major franchises, improving Entertainment visibility, and an Education plan centered on stabilization amid ongoing funding volatility.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Headwater Exploration Non-GAAP EPS of $0.49, revenue of $237.9M
data.description:
• Headwater Exploration press release (HWX:CA): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.49.
  • Revenue of $237.9M.

  • The Board of Directors of Headwater has declared a quarterly cash dividend to shareholders of $0.12 per common share payable on October 15, 2026, to shareholders of record at the close of business on September 30, 2026. This dividend is an eligible dividend for the purposes of the Income Tax Act (Canada).

data.title: Trump to hit 60 trading partners with new tariffs on Friday over forced labor
data.description:
The Trump administration is set to slap 60 trading partners, including the European Union, with new 10% and 12.5% tariffs on Friday. The penalties, imposed over alleged weak enforcement of forced labor bans, kick in just as a temporary 10% global tariff comes to an end.

The move is the White House's latest effort to restore President Donald Trump's campaign ‌vision of a near-global tariff after the U.S. Supreme Court in February struck down his "reciprocal" duties of 10% to 50% imposed last year.

The new tariffs, announced on Thursday in a Federal Register notice, will cover 99.4% of U.S. imports, but include numerous product exemptions, such as oil and gas, fertilizer and certain food items.

Imposed under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, the new duties allow the administration to maintain a tariff floor on virtually all U.S. imports despite ​the Supreme Court setback. 

“President Trump recognizes that decades of moral suasion have not eradicated forced labor from global supply chains. The United States has had a forced labor import ban for nearly a century, and rigorously enforces it; it’s well past time for our trading partners to do the same,” U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said.

Under the final determination, the U.S. will impose a 10% duty on goods of Argentina, Bangladesh, Britain, Cambodia, Canada, Ecuador, El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras, India, Indonesia, Jordan, Malaysia, Mexico, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, Trinidad and Tobago.

data.title: Friday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
9:45 AM PMI Composite Flash

The flash Composite Purchasing Managers' Index provides an early estimate of current private sector output by combining information obtained from surveys of around 1,000 manufacturing and service sector companies. 

Manufacturing is seen up at 54.3 in the July flash from 53.9 in the June final. Services are expected to remain unchanged at 51.2.

10:00 AM New Home Sales

New home sales measure the number of newly constructed homes with a committed sale during the month.

Sales are seen up from the bottom at 613K in June from a soft 580K in May.

1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count

The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs.

data.title: World Kinect forecasts full-year adjusted EPS of $3.20 to $3.40 as market volatility normalizes
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: World Kinect Corporation (WKC) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered an exceptional second quarter. Adjusted earnings per share was $1.29, the highest quarterly adjusted EPS in our company's history." (CEO & Director Ira Birns)
  • "Aviation and Marine each delivered record quarterly gross profit supported by favorable market conditions, including continued volatility associated with the conflict in the Middle East." (CEO & Director Birns)
  • "With the sale of our North American tank wagon business completed in June, we have largely accomplished the portfolio simplification objectives we set out to achieve." (CEO & Director Birns)
  • "Today, we serve approximately 3,100 retail customers, up from roughly 2,900 a year ago." (CEO & Director Birns)
  • "Total non-GAAP adjustments in the second quarter were approximately $19 million or $18 million after tax." (Executive VP & CFO Jose-Miguel Tejada)
  • "Gross profit increasing 50% year-over-year to $350 million, representing an all-time quarterly gross profit record for the company." (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

Outlook

  • "Reflecting our strong first half performance, we are again raising our full-year adjusted EPS guidance to $3.20 to $3.40 per share, up from our prior range of $2.65 to $2.85 per share." (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)
  • "While we continue to see opportunities in the current market environment, our guidance reflects a more measured level of performance in the second half of the year compared to the record first half." (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)
  • "We should also be clear that this was an exceptional quarter, and not every quarter will look like this." (CEO & Director Birns)

Financial Results

  • "On a consolidated basis, second quarter volume was down 9%, driven primarily by lower demand tied to the Middle East conflict, as well as businesses we have exited within Land." (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)
  • "Aviation volume was 1.8 billion gallons, down 5% year-over-year" and "Aviation gross profit increased 51% year-over-year to $208 million, an all-time quarterly record for the segment." (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)
  • "Services represented approximately 18% of Aviation gross profit" and "the contribution from our services businesses more than doubling year-over-year." (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)
  • "Marine volume was 3.5 million metric tons, down 10% year-over-year" and "Marine gross profit increased almost three times the prior year level to $80 million, the highest quarterly gross profit in the history of the segment." (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)
  • "Land operating income was $20 million compared with $1 million in the second quarter of last year, and operating margin expanded to over 32% of gross profit." (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)
  • "Operating cash flow for the quarter was a use of approximately $21 million and free cash flow was a use of approximately $35 million" and "the sale of our tank wagon and lubricants businesses returned approximately $85 million of cash to the business in the quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

Q&A

  • Ken Hoexter, BofA Securities: "Why such a conservative view given that setup?" Executive VP & CFO Tejada: "The markets are difficult to predict... So we've taken a more measured approach."
  • Ken Hoexter, BofA Securities: "How do we think about what is a normal run rate in terms of profit?" Executive VP & CFO Tejada: "I think thinking back to where we started the year is probably a good baseline and then seeing the opportunity from there."
  • Ken Hoexter, BofA Securities: "What's your thought on bad debt?" Executive VP & CFO Tejada: "We did tick up our reserves. There was one specific customer that sought credit protection... That was an outlier."
  • Justin Jenkins, Raymond James: "Do you think there's a new normal here even after the conflict?" CEO & Director Birns: "I wouldn't call a new normal by any means. Could margins remain elevated beyond where they were at the beginning of the year? Sure, that's possible."
  • Justin Jenkins, Raymond James: "What type of backdrop or timeframe do you think we need to see here" on working capital? Executive VP & CFO Tejada: "We've seen early signs of working capital improvement entering the third quarter" and "that we get all the way back to $200 million-plus from this point, a bit challenged."
  • Justin Jenkins, Raymond James: "Should we think of OpEx staying somewhat elevated"? CEO & Director Birns: "The second quarter was elevated because of the reasons that Mike described. Chances are that the number should be down fairly significantly in Q3 versus Q2 for sure."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive and inquisitive, pressing on sustainability and conservatism in guidance, including "Why such a conservative view" and questions about a "new normal" for volatility and margins. (Hoexter, BofA Securities; Jenkins, Raymond James)
  • Management tone was slightly positive but cautious on predictability, emphasizing normalization and risk controls: "We are not assuming the market conditions we saw over the past few months will repeat" and "The markets are difficult to predict." (CEO & Director Birns; Executive VP & CFO Tejada)
  • Compared with Q1, management repeated a similar posture of capturing upside while not embedding it into guidance, shifting from Q1 uncertainty about duration to Q2 noting that "Those conditions persisted longer than we initially forecast" while still taking a "more measured" second-half view. (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance moved higher quarter over quarter, with Q2 raising full-year adjusted EPS to "$3.20 to $3.40" from Q1’s "$2.65 to $2.85" framework, while keeping the same core message that the company is not forecasting continued elevated volatility. (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)
  • Portfolio simplification advanced from Q1’s expectation that exits would be "completed by the end of the second quarter" to Q2’s confirmation that "we completed the sale of our tank wagon delivery and lubricants businesses" and had "largely accomplished" simplification objectives. (CEO & Director Birns; Executive VP & CFO Tejada)
  • Analyst focus broadened from primarily conflict-driven volatility, seasonality, and credit lines in Q1 to Q2 emphasis on sustainability of profit run-rate, bad debt reserve levels, and timing of working-capital reversal. (Hoexter, BofA Securities; Jenkins, Raymond James)

Risks and Concerns

  • "We should also be clear that this was an exceptional quarter, and not every quarter will look like this" and "we are not assuming the market conditions we saw over the past few months will repeat." (CEO & Director Birns)
  • "A higher bad debt reserve, reflecting increased credit risk among certain customers driven by the elevated fuel prices and increased volatility" and "There was one specific customer that sought credit protection." (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)
  • "Higher commodity prices and volatility continue to place demands on working capital" with management pointing to "early signs of working capital improvement entering the third quarter" and continued proactive exposure management. (Executive VP & CFO Tejada)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q2 as a record, volatility-assisted quarter driven by execution, while stressing it is not a normal run rate. Leadership highlighted completed Land divestitures and a more focused portfolio, raised full-year adjusted EPS guidance to $3.20 to $3.40, and pointed to credit, bad-debt reserving, and working-capital demands as key areas of discipline as market conditions normalize into the second half.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Business First targets 1.25% ROAA exit run-rate as it expects high single-digit loan growth in H2 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Business First Bancshares (BFST) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "b1BANK had an encouragingly solid second quarter" with David Melville (Chairman, President & CEO) saying it "met or exceeded the progress we've been articulating" and "positions us well for a strong second half of 2026."

  • Melville highlighted balance-sheet actions and credit improvement, stating, "Margin expanded by 8 basis points during the quarter," and "we also executed a relatively sizable loan sale" that "will create additional margin opportunity as we redeploy those proceeds into higher-earning assets over the next two quarters." He added, "Our team made meaningful progress on the credit front, reducing non-performing loans by about 30%."

  • Melville called out two operational issues that could affect near-term expectations: "If there was a headline disappointment in the quarterly numbers, it was in two areas. First, deposits," and "Second, expenses ran a little higher than normal," with elevated items tied to "upfront marketing spend" and "elevated legal fees connected to the resolution of a large non-performing credit."

  • Gregory Robertson (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer) summarized reported and core earnings items: "Second quarter GAAP net income and EPS available to common shareholders was $22.8 million and $0.70" and "Excluding these non-core items, non-GAAP core net income and EPS available to common shareholders was $23.3 million and $0.71 per share."

  • Robertson emphasized profitability and capital actions: "Second quarter results marked another quarter of strong financial performance, generating a 1.05% core ROAA and a core efficiency ratio of 63.9%," and "we completed the fully self-managed private placement of $85 million of 6.5% fixed-to-floating rate subordinated debt notes due in 2036."

Outlook

  • On balance-sheet redeployment, Robertson told Matt Olney, Stephens: "we expect to pick up about 4 basis points go-forward impact to the margin in the quarter" from the late-quarter loan-sale repositioning, and David Melville (Chairman, President & CEO) added, "We continue to expect... a high single-digit increase, annualized increase in both the third and fourth quarters."

  • On expenses into 2H 2026, Robertson told Feddie Strickland, Hovde: "slightly down in the third quarter, closer to 58 in the third quarter, and then closer to 57 in the fourth quarter is the way we think," and said, "we do expect expenses to be lower as we recognize cost savings in the fourth quarter from the Progressive acquisition."

  • On rates and deposit sensitivity, Robertson said, "Our baseline assumption is that we don't receive any further interest rate cuts during 2026," and "We continue to see 45% to 55% overall deposit beta as achievable regarding any future rate cuts."

  • On year-end capital expectations, Matthew Sealy (Senior VP, Director, Corporate Strategy and FP&A) said, "consolidated total risk-based... around 13.9%," "on CET1, just under 10.6%," and "TCE, likely to reach about 9%."

Financial Results

  • Robertson said Q2 profitability included "a 1.05% core ROAA" and "a core efficiency ratio of 63.9%," and described Q2 drivers as "better-than-expected margin expansion" and "building capital levels from disciplined balance sheet management."

  • On balance sheet movement, Robertson reported, "Total loans held for investment decreased $24.8 million" but "Excluding the Progressive loan sale... total loans held for investment increased to $96.4 million." He added, "Total deposits decreased $229.4 million" with "a $237.9 million decrease in interest-bearing deposits" and "an $8.5 million increase in non-interest-bearing deposits."

  • On net interest margin and deposit costs, Robertson reported, "Our GAAP-reported second quarter net interest margin increased 8 basis points linked-quarter to 3.73%," and "the non-GAAP core net interest margin... increased 8 basis points as well from 3.60% to 3.68%." He also said, "cost of deposits decreased 7 basis points" and "Total cost of deposits... was 2.26%."

  • On non-interest line items, Robertson said, "Core non-interest expense for the second quarter was $58.4 million" and the increase was "mostly due to elevated marketing and advertising spend." He also reported, "Core non-interest income" was "primarily due to slower swap fee revenue" and noted, "some of our non-interest revenue business can be lumpy from quarter to quarter."

  • On credit, Robertson reported, "The ratio of non-performing loans compared to loans held for investment decreased... to 1.26%," and said the change was "largely driven by a resolution of certain previously identified CRE and commercial business relationships."

Q&A

  • Matt Olney, Stephens: asked about liquidity redeployment and margin impact from repositioning; Executive VP Robertson: "we expect to pick up about 4 basis points go-forward impact to the margin" and said he did not expect a "material impact" to average earning assets; Chairman Melville: "we continue to expect... a high single-digit increase... in both the third and fourth quarters" and expected to put liquidity to work "half in the third quarter and half in the fourth quarter."

  • Matt Olney, Stephens: pressed on deposits and borrowings; Executive VP Robertson: said much of the outflow was seasonal, noted paying down "slightly over $60 million in brokered" deposits "weighted average above 4%," and said inflows had already "come back in" early in Q3.

  • Feddie Strickland, Hovde: asked about the expense cadence and Progressive savings timing; Executive VP Robertson: guided to "closer to 58" in Q3 and "closer to 57" in Q4 and reiterated savings expected in Q4.

  • Feddie Strickland, Hovde: asked about buybacks; Executive VP Robertson: "We did 176,000 shares, about $4.8 million in the second quarter" and said repurchases depend on other capital uses, including "organic growth opportunities" and the "callable event of our preferred stock next year in September."

  • Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson: asked about deposit cost pressures and CDs; Executive VP Robertson: said inflows were "pretty much matching the average rate for Q2" and added, "we've got some opportunities with both brokered and organic CDs in the third and the fourth quarter to reprice those down."

  • Christopher Marinac, Brean: asked about criticized asset trends and allowance; Executive VP Robertson: targeted NPL resolutions of "10% to 20%" in Q3 and said ending the year "closer to $50 million or slightly below" was achievable, while adding, "our plan is to try to grow into what we have" on reserves.

  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: asked how Louisiana/Meta investment translates into banking opportunity; Chairman Melville: "We're certainly not camping out next door expecting to bank the data center itself," but described vendor and trickle-down opportunities, citing, "the Richland Parish School System gave each of their teachers a $50,000 bonus" as an example of broader economic effects.

  • Matt Olney, Stephens: asked about charge-offs and the 1.25% ROAA target; Executive VP Robertson: said "high single digits" in annualized charge-offs could occur in the next two quarters and added, "we're in the risk business"; Chairman Melville: said, "we still believe if we perform and execute and things go our way that, that is a credible opportunity" to reach "the 1.25% ROAA."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, focusing on deposit volatility, expense cadence, and credit outcomes, including questions on "anticipated charge-offs" (Matt Olney, Stephens) and deposit cost sensitivity (Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more detailed in Q&A, with confidence on execution items like redeployment and loan growth, including "we expect to pick up about 4 basis points" (Executive VP Robertson) and "we continue to expect... a high single-digit increase" (Chairman Melville), while acknowledging uncertainty on losses: "it's hard to say no losses" (Executive VP Robertson).

  • Versus Q1, Q2 management commentary contained fewer references to margin “noise” and more emphasis on realized credit improvement and margin expansion; Q1 included, "there was about $1.2 million in interest reversal" (Executive VP Robertson), while Q2 noted, "did not experience any interest income reversal" (Executive VP Robertson).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 shifted from acquisition-close integration and hiring momentum toward optimization and redeployment: Q1 emphasized Progressive closing and hiring, while Q2 emphasized "returned to our normalized rate of loan production" and a "relatively sizable loan sale" to lift margin (Chairman Melville).

  • Margin and credit direction improved in Q2 compared with Q1: Q1 core NIM was described as decreasing to "3.60%" and Q2 core NIM increased to "3.68%" (Executive VP Robertson), while NPL ratio moved from "1.53%" in Q1 to "1.26%" in Q2 (Executive VP Robertson).

  • Deposits swung from Q1 growth to Q2 decline: Q1 "Total deposits increased $766.4 million" (Executive VP Robertson), while Q2 "Total deposits decreased $229.4 million" (Executive VP Robertson), with Q2 management framing part of the decline as "purposeful" due to brokered paydowns (Chairman Melville).

  • Analysts’ focus remained consistent across both quarters—credit resolution timelines, margin trajectory, and expense outlook—while Q2 added more discussion on Louisiana macro tailwinds and pipeline-driven growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Deposit variability and funding mix were a key issue, with Chairman Melville citing deposits as a "headline disappointment" and Executive VP Robertson attributing Q2 declines to seasonal municipal/commercial flows and brokered deposit paydowns.

  • Expense normalization is expected but not guaranteed, with management pointing to "elevated marketing" and "elevated legal fees" (Chairman Melville) as Q2 drivers, and Executive VP Robertson providing a lower run-rate outlook into Q3 and Q4.

  • Credit resolution remains an execution and loss-recognition variable, with Executive VP Robertson warning, "we're in the risk business," while also targeting further NPL and OREO reductions and maintaining a reserve growth approach.

  • Non-interest income variability was acknowledged, as Executive VP Robertson said swap fee revenue was slower and "lumpy," and Chairman Melville described several fee businesses as "still... fairly new" and subject to quarter-to-quarter variability.

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q2 as a step toward higher structural profitability, citing an 8-basis-point margin expansion, a loan sale intended to support near-term margin lift as proceeds are redeployed, and improved credit metrics alongside planned expense normalization. Executives reiterated goals around high single-digit annualized loan growth in Q3 and Q4 and maintained the ambition of a 1.25% ROAA exit run-rate, while acknowledging key swing factors including deposit seasonality, lumpy fee income, and the inherently uncertain timing and loss outcomes of credit resolutions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
